findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ accumulates 27 files across unrelated concerns, diluting its ownership boundary
evidence: infrastructure/core/ contains 27 files with avg_fan_in of 9.3, covering logging, config, pipeline orchestration, security scanning, credentials, menu rendering, performance monitoring, retry logic, and multi-project coordination — all under the same 'core' label

Files like menu.py (interactive CLI menus), security.py (regex-based code scanning), and credentials.py (API key management) share the same namespace as foundational utilities like logging_utils.py and exceptions.py despite having completely different change drivers

The directory profile shows core/ is imported by 10+ distinct directories (scripts/, validation/, rendering/, reporting/, publishing/, llm/core/, steganography/), which means changes to any of its 27 files can ripple widely without boundary clarity
findings.suggestion: Extract thematic sub-packages from infrastructure/core/: pipeline orchestration (pipeline.py, checkpoint.py, pipeline_summary.py) into infrastructure/pipeline/, security concerns (security.py, credentials.py) into infrastructure/security/, and UI concerns (menu.py, progress.py) into infrastructure/ui/. Keep core/ for only the genuinely shared primitives (logging, exceptions, config, file_operations).

findings.summary: validate_markdown_cli.py implements domain logic in a file declared as a thin orchestrator
evidence: The file's own docstring says 'All business logic is in infrastructure/markdown_validator.py / This script handles only CLI argument parsing and I/O' yet the file directly implements find_markdown_files(), collect_symbols(), validate_images(), validate_refs(), and validate_math() — each containing independent regex parsing and file I/O logic
find_markdown_files() implements a custom numeric sort (lines 67-78) using filter(str.isalpha) and filter(str.isdigit) — this is domain logic, not CLI wiring
validate_images() and validate_refs() open files, apply regex, and build issue lists — all pure logic that belongs in the validator module, not in the CLI layer

sys.path.insert(0, ...) at module level (line 22) indicates the file is also used as an executable script, yet it re-exports functions explicitly 'for tests' — it plays three roles simultaneously (CLI entry, library, test helper)
findings.suggestion: Move find_markdown_files, collect_symbols, validate_images, validate_refs, and validate_math into infrastructure/validation/markdown_validator.py where the declared logic lives. Keep validate_markdown_cli.py as a pure CLI shim (argument parsing + calling validate_markdown()).

findings.summary: validate_build_artifacts() in integrity.py hardcodes a specific project's file manifest, breaking the generic infrastructure contract
evidence: infrastructure/validation/integrity.py lines 461-503: the default expected_structure dict hardcodes filenames like 'ablation_study.png', 'convergence_plot.png', 'image_classification_results.png', 'recommendation_scalability.png' — all specific to the code_project optimizer project

The module docstring claims generic reusability ('Part of the infrastructure layer (Layer 1) - reusable across all projects') but the default artifact list is project-specific, meaning any other project calling this function with no arguments will fail validation against wrong expected files
The steganography module, reporting module, and multi-project summary all live in infrastructure/ and would legitimately call this function — none of them produce these filenames
findings.suggestion: Remove the hardcoded default expected_structure. Make expected_files a required parameter, or derive defaults from project config (e.g., scanning what actually exists in output/) rather than embedding project-specific filenames in Layer 1 infrastructure.

findings.summary: verify_academic_standards() pre-populates a dict then overwrites every key unconditionally, making the initializer noise
evidence: Lines 208-219: standards dict is initialized with 10 explicit key-value pairs (has_abstract: False, has_introduction: False, etc.)
Lines 234-274: each key is then unconditionally reassigned via regex search results — the initial values in the dict literal are never read before being overwritten

The existing_findings in the packet confirms this: 'dict_keys: Dict key has_abstract overwritten on standards at line 234 (previously set at ...) — same pattern for has_introduction, has_methodology, has_results, has_discussion, has_conclusion, has_references, proper_citations, equation_labels, figure_captions'
The pre-populated dict creates false documentation value (readers may think defaults matter) while actually being dead initialization
findings.suggestion: Build the standards dict via direct assignment or a single dict comprehension from a defined set of checked keys. Alternatively, define a dataclass/TypedDict so defaults carry semantic meaning. Eliminate the two-pass init-then-overwrite pattern.

findings.summary: LLMClient.query() logs entry/exit and intermediate state at 5 separate points for a single call, burying the seam contract in telemetry
evidence: Lines 188-196: logger.info('Resetting context', extra={...}) with 3 fields logged when reset_context=True
Lines 200-207: logger.debug('System prompt re-injected after context reset', extra={...})
Lines 210-222: logger.info('Starting query', extra={...}) logs 7 fields including prompt_preview, context_tokens_est, temperature, seed
Lines 225-232: logger.debug('Added user message to context', extra={...}) with 3 fields
Lines 239-250: logger.info('Query completed', extra={...}) logs 5 more fields including response_preview
Lines 253-260: logger.debug('Added assistant message to context', extra={...}) with 3 more fields
This is 6 log statements for a single query() call, most encoding internal implementation details (context_tokens_est, message_length) rather than boundary contract information
findings.suggestion: Collapse to one info-level log at query start (model, prompt_length) and one at completion (generation_time, response_length). Move internal state logging (context tokens, message counts) to a dedicated debug context manager or metrics object rather than scattering across the call body.

findings.summary: clean_output_directory() uses three-tier exception handling (PermissionError, OSError, Exception) that duplicates the broad-except anti-pattern it is trying to avoid
evidence: Lines 38-46: three successive except blocks for PermissionError, OSError, and Exception, each logging independently and returning False — the bare except Exception catches OSError anyway, making the OSError branch unreachable in practice after Python 3.3 (PermissionError IS an OSError subclass)
Lines 60-67: identical pattern repeated for the directory removal branch
The same three-tier pattern appears twice in the same function body, suggesting copy-paste rather than a deliberate error taxonomy
findings.suggestion: Collapse to a single 'except OSError as e' (which covers PermissionError). The bare 'except Exception' fallback adds no value when OSError already covers all filesystem errors; remove it or restrict it to genuinely unexpected exceptions and log with exc_info=True.

findings.summary: validate_publication_readiness() reads and concatenates all markdown file content to do keyword presence checks, coupling a high-level readiness judgment to raw file scanning
evidence: Lines 263-275: all_content is built by concatenating raw file reads, then 'abstract in all_content.lower()' etc. are used to infer document structure

The section detection logic ('abstract', 'introduction', 'methodology', 'results', 'conclusion') duplicates the regex-based checks already present in infrastructure/validation/integrity.py's verify_academic_standards() at lines 234-252 which uses proper regex anchors like r'\\section\{.*abstract|#\s*abstract'
The publishing module re-implements a stripped version of validation logic that already exists in the validation module, creating two divergent paths for the same structural check
findings.suggestion: Replace the ad-hoc keyword scan in validate_publication_readiness() with a call to infrastructure.validation.integrity.verify_academic_standards(), which already performs the same check with proper regex anchoring. This removes the duplicated content-scanning logic from the publishing boundary.

findings.summary: format_author_name() has a branch for single vs. multiple authors that both return the same expression
evidence: Lines 204-210: 'if len(authors) == 1: return authors[0].get("name", "Project Author")' followed immediately by 'return authors[0].get("name", "Project Author")' — the single-author branch and the multi-author fallthrough return the identical expression

The comment 'For multiple authors, return first author name' suggests an intent to format differently (e.g., 'Author et al.') but that logic was never implemented, leaving a dead branch that adds noise without behavioral distinction
findings.suggestion: Collapse to a single return after the empty-list guard: 'return authors[0].get("name", "Project Author")'. If multi-author formatting is genuinely needed, implement it (e.g., add 'et al.' for len > 1); otherwise remove the misleading branch.

findings.summary: verify_data_consistency() uses a function-level import of numpy inside a conditional branch, creating inconsistent import style relative to the module
evidence: Line 182: 'import numpy as np' inside the elif branch for .npz/.npy files — this is a runtime import hidden inside business logic
The module imports json, os, pickle, re, and Path at the top, but numpy is imported conditionally inside the function body

The existing findings note 6 function-level imports flagged as 'possible circular import workaround' across infrastructure/core/logging_progress.py — the same pattern here is inconsistent with the top-level import convention the rest of the module follows
findings.suggestion: Move 'import numpy as np' to the top of the file, guarded by a try/except ImportError with NUMPY_AVAILABLE flag, matching the pattern used for yaml in config_loader.py (lines 93-98). Then gate the .npz/.npy validation branch on NUMPY_AVAILABLE.

findings.summary: _setup_log_file_handler() silently swallows exceptions when closing the previous log handler
evidence: Lines 131-135: 'if self._log_handler is not None: try: self._log_handler.close() except Exception: pass' — bare except with pass, no logging of what failed

This is in a method that is called both at init and after the clean stage runs, meaning a handler close failure during a pipeline run is silently ignored with no indication of why log output may subsequently be missing
The existing findings for pipeline.py flag '1x Empty except block (except: pass)' confirming this is a real occurrence
findings.suggestion: Replace 'except Exception: pass' with at minimum 'except Exception as e: print(f"Warning: could not close log handler: {e}", file=sys.stderr)' since the logger itself may be in an undefined state at this point. Alternatively log to a fallback handler or re-raise if handler close failure is truly unrecoverable.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/environment.py contains 13 top-level functions across 12 disconnected clusters with no unifying abstraction
evidence: The existing_findings confirm: 'responsibility_cohesion: Mixed responsibilities: 13 top-level funcs across 12 disconnected clusters (2, 1, ...)'

The module contains: check_python_version, check_dependencies, install_missing_packages, check_build_tools, setup_directories, check_uv_available, get_python_command, validate_interpreter, set_environment_variables, get_subprocess_env, validate_uv_sync_result, validate_directory_structure, check_environment_health — each a free-standing function with no shared state or coherent API grouping

The module docstring lists 6 distinct capability areas: uv integration, dependency validation, build tool checking, directory setup, env var configuration, and post-setup validation — each a separate concern that would merit its own module in a cohesive design
Callers must import individual functions from a grab-bag rather than interacting with a focused API (e.g., an EnvironmentValidator class or separate setup vs. validation modules)
findings.suggestion: Split into at minimum two modules: infrastructure/core/env_setup.py (functions that mutate environment: setup_directories, set_environment_variables, install_missing_packages) and infrastructure/core/env_check.py (pure predicates: check_python_version, check_dependencies, check_build_tools, check_uv_available, validate_interpreter). This localizes change and makes the API surface readable.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/dashboard_generator.py is a 3496-LOC module with 24 top-level functions across 9 disconnected clusters, indicating deep responsibility confusion
evidence: The existing_findings confirm: 'structural: Needs decomposition: large (3496 LOC) / complexity score 266' and 'responsibility_cohesion: Mixed responsibilities: 24 top-level funcs across 9 disconnected clusters'
The module mixes matplotlib chart generation, HTML dashboard templating, manuscript overview generation, file organization, and executive summary integration — all in one flat file

The import block alone spans matplotlib, numpy, csv, plus internal modules: ExecutiveSummary, ProjectMetrics, generate_all_manuscript_overviews, FileType, OutputOrganizer — pulling in the entire reporting subsystem as a monolith
21 catch blocks that only log (swallowed errors per existing findings) spread across the file indicates no coherent error contract
findings.suggestion: Decompose into chart_generators.py (matplotlib figure functions), html_dashboard.py (HTML template assembly), and a thin dashboard.py orchestrator that calls both. Each resulting module should be under 400 LOC with a single stated responsibility.

findings.summary: get_config_as_dict() and get_config_as_env_vars() are two public functions in config_loader.py that differ only by an os.environ filter, not distinct responsibilities
evidence: Lines 213-281: get_config_as_dict() returns Dict[str, str] of config values; get_config_as_env_vars() calls get_config_as_dict() and optionally filters out keys already in os.environ

The entire body of get_config_as_env_vars() is: 'config_dict = get_config_as_dict(repo_root); if respect_existing: return {k: v for k, v in config_dict.items() if k not in os.environ}; else: return config_dict'

This is a pass-through wrapper with a filter: the 'else' branch just returns the result of the inner call unchanged, meaning get_config_as_env_vars(repo_root, respect_existing=False) is exactly get_config_as_dict(repo_root) with extra function call overhead
Both functions appear in the public API of config_loader.py, doubling the surface for what is logically one operation with one optional parameter
findings.suggestion: Collapse get_config_as_env_vars() into get_config_as_dict() via an optional 'respect_existing: bool = False' parameter, or eliminate get_config_as_env_vars() entirely and let callers do the os.environ filter inline (it is a one-liner). Remove the wrapper function to keep the module's public surface minimal.
